questions.prompt: Put the six offboarding steps in the order documented in the lesson.
items: Pause new alert notifications for the customer's organisation.
Bulk uninstall agents via the RMM.
Archive or export incidents per retention policy.
Raise the billing-closure ticket for the senior.
questions.explanation: Pause first so subsequent steps don't fan out to the customer; ITDR before agents (bigger blast radius); SIEM after the sources are quiet; archive after the platform is quiet; billing last because every prior step is reversible.

questions.prompt: "Uninstalling agents is offboarding." Why does the lesson call this out as a misconception?
choices: Uninstall is one of six steps.

choices.label: Uninstall is part of offboarding but only if the customer specifically asks; otherwise leave agents installed.
choices.rationale: Bulk uninstall is step 3 on every offboarding, not conditional on the customer's request.

choices.label: Uninstall is the last step, not the first; the misconception is about ordering.
choices.rationale: Uninstall is step 3, not last. Billing closure is last.

choices.label: Uninstalling agents triggers an automatic ITDR disconnect.
choices.rationale: The two are independent and must be disconnected separately.

questions.explanation: Doing it alone leaves ITDR live, SIEM ingesting, notifications fanning out, and the org commercially open. Offboarding is a sequence, not a single technical action. Half-done offboardings are the most common state to inherit.

questions.prompt: Why is "pause new alert notifications" the first step rather than a parallel housekeeping task?
choices: Every step after this generates signals, ITDR disconnect, bulk uninstall, SIEM disconnect.

choices.label: Huntress requires the pause as a hard gate before allowing any other offboarding action.
choices.rationale: Not a platform-enforced gate.

choices.label: Pausing alerts also disconnects ITDR automatically.
choices.rationale: The two are independent steps.

choices.label: It's a billing-side step the senior wants done early.
choices.rationale: Pause is technical, not billing. Billing closure is step 6.

questions.explanation: Without the pause, those signals fan out to the customer who has just left. The pause costs 30 seconds; the cleanup if you skip it costs a customer-relationship apology.

questions.prompt: The lesson says "the order is not a preference; it is the way each step makes the next one cleaner." Which pairing illustrates that?
choices: ITDR is disconnected before bulk agent uninstall so the bigger-blast-radius identity surface closes first;

choices.label: Billing closure runs first so the senior knows you're starting.
choices.rationale: Billing closure is last because every prior step is reversible and billing is the irreversible commercial step.

choices.label: SIEM is disconnected first because it's the smallest surface.
choices.rationale: SIEM is step 4, not first.

choices.label: Incident archive runs first so you have a record before doing anything.
choices.rationale: Archive is step 5, after the platform has gone quiet, so the export captures a stable state.

questions.explanation: The agent uninstall then runs without identity events landing during the window. Identity (bigger blast radius) before endpoints (long-tail surface) is the lesson's framing.

questions.prompt: The account manager messages you: "We lost the customer, they're moving providers. Please get them offboarded by Friday." What do you do first?

choices.label: Schedule the bulk-uninstall job for Friday.
choices.rationale: Bulk uninstall is step 3 of 6. Starting there means steps 1 and 2 didn't happen.

choices: Confirm the offboarding with the senior.

choices.label: Delete the customer from the portal so they're "definitely gone."
choices.rationale: Permanent org deletion is not on the sequence; the senior owns it; the rest of the offboarding becomes harder.

questions.explanation: (the account manager isn't necessarily the authorising senior). Then start at step 1: pause notifications. Two things at once: verify authority (the account manager is messaging, not necessarily authorising), and start the sequence at step 1.

questions.prompt: You've paused alerts, disconnected ITDR, and bulk-uninstalled. Portal shows 4 of 198 agents still online (resistant). Friday is tomorrow. The account manager pings: "Are we done? Can I tell them they're off?"

choices.label: Tell the account manager yes; 194 of 198 is close enough.
choices.rationale: The four are still phoning home. The sequence has more steps. 'Close enough' isn't the standard.

choices: Tell the account manager what's left.

choices.label: Push the bulk-uninstall job a second time on the four, then declare done.
choices.rationale: Resistant agents need per-endpoint follow-up, not bulk re-push. And SIEM, archive, and billing are still left.

questions.explanation: Four resistant agents, SIEM disconnect (if applicable), incident archive, billing-closure ticket. Give a realistic estimate (maybe Monday). Don't declare done until done. The account manager's job is managing the customer's expectations; you give them the real picture.

questions.prompt: "Deleting the org is the final step." Why is this misconception worth correcting?
choices: Permanent org deletion is not on the sequence at all.

choices.label: Org deletion is the final step but the senior runs it, not the tech.
choices.rationale: Deletion is not on the standard sequence at all, even seniors usually leave the org archived for years.

choices.label: Deletion is step 7, the lesson just doesn't mention it explicitly.
choices.rationale: There are six steps, not seven.

choices.label: Deletion is automated by Huntress 90 days after billing closure.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: The senior owns the decision, usually weeks or months later; most MSPs leave the org archived for record-retention. Don't conflate offboarding with deletion. Deletion is irreversible and senior-owned, usually later.

questions.prompt: Why is the billing-closure ticket raised by the tech but not closed by the tech?
choices: The senior owns the commercial wrap-up.

choices.label: Closing the ticket requires senior credentials in the PSA.
choices.rationale: Not necessarily a permissions issue; the reason is scope of authority over commercial decisions.

choices.label: Closing the ticket triggers the agent uninstall.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the workflow.

choices.label: Tech-closed billing tickets bypass the audit log.
choices.rationale: Not a real workflow constraint; the reason is ownership.

questions.explanation: Subscription cancellation with Huntress, contractual closure. The call on whether to permanently deactivate the org later. The tech raises and routes; the senior owns the close. Same boundary lesson 1 opened with.

questions.prompt: "I can skip the pause-alerts step if I'm fast." What's the lesson's response?
choices: No, the other steps generate signals;

choices.label: Yes, if the rest of the sequence happens within an hour, the pause is unnecessary.
choices.rationale: Inverts the lesson. There's no 'speed exempts you' rule.

choices.label: Yes, Huntress holds alerts for 24 hours after the last sync, so they won't fan out anyway.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

choices.label: Only on customers with fewer than 50 endpoints.
choices.rationale: No size-based exemption.

questions.explanation: Without the pause, those signals reach the customer who has left. The pause costs 30 seconds; the cleanup costs a customer-relationship apology. The pause is the cheapest insurance in the sequence.

questions.prompt: Lifting the partner-level branding from the customer's organisation is one of the six documented offboarding steps.
questions.explanation: Branding usually inherits from the partner level and doesn't need per-customer cleanup. Not on the standard sequence.
